name: Active Scanning
description: Adversaries execute active reconnaissance scans to gather information that can be used during targeting. This includes probing victim infrastructure directly via port scans, vulnerability scans, and banner grabbing using tools such as Nmap, Masscan, and Nessus. Active scanning generates network traffic that may be logged by IDS/IPS or firewall systems, distinguishing it from passive reconnaissance. Attackers may scan from compromised infrastructure or cloud VPS to avoid attribution, and often combine results with OSINT to build a comprehensive target profile.

name: Scanning IP Blocks
description: Adversaries scan victim IP blocks using tools like Masscan, Zmap, or Nmap to identify live hosts, open ports, and active services across entire CIDR ranges. This broad-spectrum scanning helps map the external attack surface and identify internet-facing assets that may be vulnerable to exploitation.

name: Vulnerability Scanning
description: Adversaries run vulnerability scanners such as Nessus, OpenVAS, Nuclei, or Qualys against victim infrastructure to discover exploitable weaknesses. These scans identify missing patches, misconfigurations, default credentials, and known CVEs that can be weaponized for initial access or privilege escalation.

name: Gather Victim Host Information
description: Adversaries gather detailed information about victim hosts including hardware specifications, installed software, patch levels, and OS versions. This intelligence is gathered through active scanning, social engineering, or passive OSINT sources such as job postings that reveal technology stacks. Knowledge of host configurations helps adversaries select appropriate exploits, tailor payloads to the target environment, and avoid triggering security controls specific to certain platforms.

name: Gather Victim Identity Information
description: Adversaries collect information about victim identities including employee names, email addresses, phone numbers, roles, and credentials from breached databases. Sources include LinkedIn scraping, data broker sites, OSINT tools like theHarvester and Hunter.io, and leaked credential databases on dark web marketplaces. This information enables targeted spearphishing campaigns, credential stuffing attacks, social engineering, and helps adversaries identify high-value targets such as system administrators or executives.

name: Credentials
description: Adversaries gather credentials from public sources such as breach dumps (Have I Been Pwned), paste sites, dark web markets, and infostealer logs. These stolen credentials are tested against target login portals using automated tools for credential stuffing, or used directly if passwords are reused across services.

name: Gather Victim Network Information
description: Adversaries gather information about the victim's network architecture including IP ranges, domain names, DNS records, BGP routing, CDN usage, and cloud provider details. Tools such as dig, whois, Shodan, Censys, and SecurityTrails reveal network topology, hosting relationships, and exposed services. This intelligence helps plan network infiltration paths, identify internet-facing entry points, and understand internal segmentation that may be encountered post-compromise.

name: Gather Victim Org Information
description: Adversaries gather organizational intelligence about the victim including business operations, key personnel, organizational hierarchy, physical locations, M&A activity, and technology partnerships. Sources include corporate websites, SEC filings, press releases, LinkedIn, and social engineering. This information supports targeted social engineering campaigns, identifies trusted relationships that can be exploited, and helps understand the victim's security posture and budget priorities.

name: Search Open Websites/Domains
description: Adversaries search freely available websites, forums, code repositories, and domains for information about victims that can inform targeting. This includes Google dorking, GitHub searches for leaked secrets, Pastebin monitoring, and searching technical forums where employees discuss infrastructure. Exposed API keys, internal documentation, server configurations, and employee discussions on Stack Overflow can reveal critical details about the target environment.

name: Social Media
description: Adversaries mine social media platforms such as LinkedIn, Twitter/X, Facebook, and Instagram to build profiles of target employees, identify organizational structure, discover technology stacks from job postings, and craft convincing social engineering pretexts using personal information shared publicly.

name: Search Victim-Owned Websites
description: Adversaries examine victim-owned websites including corporate sites, career portals, partner pages, and documentation portals to discover technology stacks, employee names, email formats, organizational structure, and exposed administrative interfaces. Technologies can be fingerprinted from HTTP headers, JavaScript libraries, and HTML comments left by developers.

name: Search Closed Sources
description: Adversaries search closed or restricted-access sources for victim information, including dark web forums, underground markets, threat intelligence feeds, private Telegram channels, and paid data broker services. These sources may contain stolen credentials, internal documents from previous breaches, zero-day exploit listings, or access-as-a-service offerings specifically targeting the victim organization.

name: Search Open Technical Databases
description: Adversaries query publicly available technical databases such as Shodan, Censys, VirusTotal, DNSdumpster, and certificate transparency logs to discover exposed services, SSL certificate details, historical DNS records, and technology fingerprints. These databases aggregate scan data from the entire internet and can reveal assets the victim organization may not realize are publicly visible, including forgotten dev servers, IoT devices, and misconfigured cloud instances.

name: Acquire Infrastructure
description: Adversaries purchase, rent, or otherwise acquire infrastructure for use during targeting, including virtual private servers, domains, cloud compute instances, and bulletproof hosting. This infrastructure serves as staging areas for payloads, command-and-control (C2) servers, phishing landing pages, and exfiltration endpoints. Adversaries often use cryptocurrency for payment and register through anonymizing services to avoid attribution. Advanced actors maintain large pools of rotating infrastructure to evade blocklisting and takedown efforts.

name: Domains
description: Adversaries acquire domain names that closely resemble legitimate organizations (typosquatting, homograph attacks) or use generic names for phishing campaigns, watering holes, C2 infrastructure, and payload hosting. They may use privacy-protecting registrars, recently expired domains with residual trust, or aged domains to bypass reputation filters.

name: Web Services
description: Adversaries register accounts on legitimate web services such as GitHub, Google Drive, Cloudflare Workers, Azure Functions, Slack, or Telegram for use as C2 channels, data exfiltration endpoints, or payload staging. Traffic to these services blends with legitimate usage and is often TLS-encrypted, making detection particularly challenging.

name: Compromise Infrastructure
description: Adversaries compromise third-party servers, websites, and network devices to use as operational infrastructure rather than purchasing their own. This includes hacking web servers for phishing or watering hole delivery, compromising email servers for spam campaigns, and taking over IoT devices for C2 relaying or DDoS. Using compromised infrastructure provides plausible deniability and makes attribution significantly more difficult since the traffic originates from legitimate organizations.

name: Develop Capabilities
description: Adversaries invest resources in developing custom capabilities including malware, exploits, tools, and digital certificates for use in operations. Custom-developed tools are harder to detect than known malware because they lack existing signatures and may evade behavioral heuristics. Nation-state actors and sophisticated criminal groups maintain dedicated development teams that create bespoke implants, zero-day exploits, and specialized tools tailored for specific target environments.

name: Malware
description: Adversaries develop custom malware families including RATs, backdoors, ransomware, wipers, and credential stealers tailored to their operational needs. Custom malware may incorporate anti-analysis techniques, environmental checks to thwart sandbox detonation, and modular architectures that allow deployment of additional capabilities post-infection.

name: Obtain Capabilities
description: Adversaries acquire tools, malware, exploits, and digital certificates from external sources rather than developing them in-house. Sources include open-source offensive frameworks (Cobalt Strike, Metasploit, Sliver), dark web marketplaces, exploit brokers, and crimeware-as-a-service platforms. Obtaining ready-made capabilities reduces development time and allows less sophisticated actors to conduct advanced operations. Shared tooling across multiple threat groups complicates attribution efforts.

name: Malware
description: Adversaries obtain malware from dark web markets, crimeware forums, or open-source repositories. This includes purchasing commodity RATs, ransomware builders, infostealers, and loader/dropper frameworks that can be customized for specific campaigns without requiring in-house development expertise.

name: Exploits
description: Adversaries obtain exploits for known and zero-day vulnerabilities from exploit databases like Exploit-DB, broker marketplaces, or underground forums. Purchased exploits may target widely-deployed software such as Microsoft Exchange, Apache, or VPN appliances and can be weaponized quickly for mass exploitation campaigns.

name: Vulnerabilities
description: Adversaries obtain vulnerability intelligence from public advisories (NVD, vendor bulletins), private broker networks, or zero-day markets. They use this information to develop exploits, prioritize targets running vulnerable software, and time attacks to coincide with patch release windows when defenders are most exposed.

name: Establish Accounts
description: Adversaries create accounts on email providers, social media platforms, code repositories, and cloud services to support operations. These accounts enable social engineering, build false personas for spearphishing, provide infrastructure for C2 communications via legitimate services, and create sock puppet identities for reconnaissance. Adversaries often age accounts to build credibility and reputation before using them in attacks.

name: Compromise Accounts
description: Adversaries compromise existing accounts belonging to real people or organizations rather than creating new ones. Hijacked accounts on email, social media, or cloud platforms inherit the legitimate owner's reputation and trust relationships, making social engineering and phishing significantly more convincing. Compromised accounts may be obtained through credential stuffing, phishing, or purchased from access brokers.

name: Phishing
description: Adversaries send deceptive electronic communications (email, SMS, messaging apps) to trick users into executing malicious code, revealing credentials, or enabling remote access. Phishing remains the most common initial access vector, used in over 80% of reported incidents. Modern phishing campaigns leverage sophisticated social engineering, brand impersonation, compromised email threads (conversation hijacking), QR codes, and adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) proxy kits that can bypass MFA. Defenses include email filtering, link protection, user awareness training, and FIDO2 phishing-resistant authentication.

name: Spearphishing Attachment
description: Adversaries send targeted emails with malicious attachments such as weaponized Office documents (macro-enabled), ISO/IMG disk images that bypass Mark-of-the-Web, password-protected ZIP archives, OneNote files with embedded scripts, or shortcut (.lnk) files disguised as legitimate documents. The attachments exploit user trust and may bypass email filters through encryption or novel file formats.

name: Spearphishing Link
description: Adversaries send targeted emails containing malicious URLs that direct victims to credential harvesting pages, malware downloads, or adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) proxy pages. Links may use URL shorteners, open redirects on trusted domains, or Cloudflare Turnstile challenges to evade automated link scanning by email security gateways.

name: Spearphishing via Service
description: Adversaries send phishing messages via third-party services such as LinkedIn InMail, Slack, Microsoft Teams, Discord, or job recruitment platforms where email security controls are not applied. These channels may have lower user suspicion and bypass traditional email filtering infrastructure.

name: Spearphishing Voice
description: Adversaries use voice calls (vishing) to manipulate victims into performing actions such as installing remote access software, navigating to malicious URLs, or divulging credentials and MFA codes. AI-generated voice deepfakes and caller ID spoofing increase the effectiveness of these attacks.

name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
description: Adversaries exploit vulnerabilities in internet-facing applications including web servers, VPN gateways, email servers, firewalls, and load balancers to gain initial access. Common targets include critical CVEs in Microsoft Exchange (ProxyShell, ProxyLogon), Fortinet SSL VPN, Citrix ADC, MOVEit Transfer, Confluence, and Log4j-vulnerable applications. Attackers often weaponize newly disclosed vulnerabilities within hours of public disclosure, making rapid patching essential. Exploitation typically provides a web shell or reverse shell for persistent remote access.

name: External Remote Services
description: Adversaries leverage external-facing remote services such as VPNs (Cisco AnyConnect, Pulse Secure), virtual desktop infrastructure (Citrix, VMware Horizon), RDP gateways, and SSH endpoints to gain initial network access using stolen or brute-forced credentials. These services are designed for legitimate remote access and therefore may not trigger alerts when used with valid credentials. Attackers frequently target organizations that lack MFA on remote access portals, or use MFA bypass techniques such as token theft and push notification fatigue.

name: Supply Chain Compromise
description: Adversaries compromise the supply chain by manipulating software, hardware, or service delivery mechanisms before they reach the end consumer. Notable examples include the SolarWinds Orion backdoor (SUNBURST), Kaseya VSA ransomware attack, 3CX Desktop App compromise, and npm/PyPI package poisoning campaigns. Supply chain attacks are devastating because they abuse the implicit trust organizations place in their vendors and can provide access to thousands of victims simultaneously through a single compromise point.

name: Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools
description: Adversaries inject malicious code into software dependencies (npm, PyPI, Maven, NuGet) or compromise development tools and build systems. Techniques include dependency confusion, typosquatting legitimate package names, and contributing malicious pull requests to open-source projects that are widely consumed downstream.

name: Compromise Software Supply Chain
description: Adversaries compromise the software build or distribution process itself, injecting malicious code into legitimate software updates that are signed and distributed through trusted channels. This was demonstrated in the SolarWinds and 3CX attacks where build pipelines were compromised to distribute backdoored updates to thousands of organizations.

name: Trusted Relationship
description: Adversaries exploit trusted third-party relationships to gain access to the primary target, including managed service providers (MSPs), IT outsourcing vendors, legal firms, or technology partners with VPN or API access to the victim's network. The compromise of a single MSP can provide access to hundreds of downstream client networks. Trust relationships are particularly dangerous because they often bypass normal security controls and firewall rules through pre-established connectivity.

name: Valid Accounts
description: Adversaries obtain and abuse legitimate credentials of existing accounts to gain initial access, move laterally, and maintain persistence. Credentials may be acquired through phishing, credential dumping, brute force, purchase from access brokers, or harvesting from breached databases. Valid account usage is extremely difficult to detect because it mimics legitimate user behavior and does not trigger signature-based alerts. Defenders must rely on behavioral analytics, impossible travel detection, and anomalous access pattern identification.

name: Default Accounts
description: Adversaries exploit default credentials that ship with hardware, software, or cloud services and have not been changed by administrators. Publicly documented default credentials for network devices, databases, IoT products, and management consoles are compiled in resources like DefaultCreds-cheat-sheet and routinely tested during reconnaissance.

name: Domain Accounts
description: Adversaries obtain Active Directory domain account credentials through phishing, credential dumping, Kerberoasting, or purchase from access brokers. Domain accounts provide access across multiple systems in the environment and are essential for lateral movement, especially when combined with knowledge of group memberships and trust relationships.

name: Local Accounts
description: Adversaries obtain local account credentials to access individual systems. Local administrator accounts are particularly valuable because the same password is often reused across multiple hosts (especially pre-LAPS environments), enabling lateral movement through pass-the-hash or direct authentication.

name: Cloud Accounts
description: Adversaries obtain cloud account credentials (AWS IAM, Azure AD/Entra ID, GCP) through phishing, token theft, infostealer malware, or misconfigured CI/CD pipelines that expose keys. Cloud credentials can provide access to compute resources, storage, databases, and administrative consoles without ever touching the corporate network.

name: Drive-by Compromise
description: Adversaries compromise legitimate websites to deliver exploits or malicious content to visitors during normal browsing (watering hole attacks). The victim's browser, plugins, or operating system are exploited simply by visiting the compromised page, requiring no user interaction beyond navigation. Drive-by attacks frequently target industry-specific websites visited by employees of the intended victim organization, and may use browser zero-days or exploit kits that fingerprint visitors before delivering payloads.

name: Hardware Additions
description: Adversaries introduce rogue hardware devices into the target environment to gain network access, intercept communications, or establish persistent backdoors. This includes USB keystroke injectors (Rubber Ducky, O.MG Cable), rogue WiFi access points (WiFi Pineapple), network implants (LAN Turtle, Packet Squirrel), and miniaturized computers (Raspberry Pi). Physical access to offices, server rooms, or network closets enables deployment during social engineering visits, maintenance windows, or insider operations.

name: Replication Through Removable Media
description: Adversaries spread malware via removable media including USB flash drives, external hard drives, and SD cards. Malware may use autorun functionality, disguised shortcut files, or exploitation of USB driver vulnerabilities. This technique is particularly effective against air-gapped networks and industrial control systems that are not connected to the internet, as demonstrated by Stuxnet's propagation via USB drives targeting Iranian nuclear facilities.

name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
description: Adversaries abuse command-line interfaces and scripting interpreters built into operating systems and applications to execute commands, scripts, and binaries. This is the foundation of most post-exploitation activity, enabling attackers to download additional payloads, modify system configurations, enumerate the environment, and interact with C2 frameworks. Common interpreters include PowerShell, cmd.exe, Bash, Python, JavaScript, and VBScript. Defenders monitor for suspicious interpreter process creation, unusual command-line arguments, and encoded/obfuscated script execution.

name: PowerShell
description: Adversaries abuse PowerShell for execution, reconnaissance, credential harvesting, and post-exploitation using its deep integration with .NET and Windows APIs. Techniques include encoded commands (-EncodedCommand), AMSI bypass, constrained language mode evasion, in-memory execution via reflection, and fileless malware delivery. PowerShell is used by nearly every major offensive framework including Cobalt Strike, Empire, and PowerSploit.

name: Windows Command Shell
description: Adversaries abuse cmd.exe to execute commands, run batch scripts, and chain operations using pipes and redirectors. While less capable than PowerShell, cmd.exe has lower visibility to some EDR products, generates less telemetry, and can be used to stage more sophisticated execution techniques.

name: Unix Shell
description: Adversaries abuse Unix shells (bash, sh, zsh) on Linux and macOS for command execution, scripting, and post-exploitation. Reverse shells, cron job persistence, SSH key manipulation, and system enumeration are commonly performed through shell interpreters on compromised Unix-based servers and endpoints.

name: Visual Basic
description: Adversaries abuse VBScript and VBA macros embedded in Office documents for initial execution on Windows systems. Despite Microsoft's efforts to block macros by default, attackers use mark-of-the-web bypass techniques, template injection, and social engineering to convince users to enable content in weaponized documents.

name: Python
description: Adversaries leverage Python interpreters for execution and post-exploitation, particularly on Linux servers and development machines where Python is commonly installed. Python's extensive standard library and third-party packages enable network operations, process manipulation, and encryption without requiring additional tools.

name: JavaScript
description: Adversaries abuse JavaScript engines (Node.js, Windows Script Host, browser engines) to execute malicious scripts. JScript/WScript files (.js, .wsf) on Windows can access COM objects and filesystem APIs, while server-side JavaScript on Node.js provides full system access for post-exploitation activities.

name: Exploitation for Client Execution
description: Adversaries exploit software vulnerabilities in client applications to execute code on victim endpoints. Targeted applications include web browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox), document processors (Microsoft Office, Adobe Reader), email clients, and media players. Exploits may be delivered through malicious websites, email attachments, or shared documents. Zero-day browser exploits are highly valued in the exploit market and have been used in targeted campaigns by nation-state actors against journalists, activists, and government officials.

name: User Execution
description: Adversaries rely on social engineering to trick users into executing malicious payloads by opening files, clicking links, or running programs. This is the human element of the attack chain that bridges delivery to execution. Techniques include disguising malware as legitimate documents, using trusted file-sharing platforms, impersonating IT helpdesk with urgent requests, and exploiting curiosity or fear through themed lures (invoices, legal threats, delivery notifications). User execution remains effective because it bypasses technical controls by exploiting human psychology.

name: Malicious Link
description: Adversaries rely on users clicking malicious links that redirect to credential harvesting pages, drive-by download sites, or pages that trigger browser exploits. Links may be obfuscated using URL shorteners, open redirects on trusted domains, or HTML smuggling techniques that construct payloads client-side.

name: Malicious File
description: Adversaries rely on users opening malicious files including macro-enabled Office documents, ISO/IMG disk images, LNK shortcut files, HTML application (HTA) files, and executable payloads disguised with document icons. These are typically delivered via email attachments or file-sharing links.

name: Native API
description: Adversaries interact directly with the native operating system API rather than through higher-level interfaces to execute behaviors, evade detection, and manipulate system internals. On Windows, this includes using ntdll.dll for direct syscalls to bypass EDR hooks on kernel32.dll and user32.dll, calling NtCreateThread and NtAllocateVirtualMemory for process injection, and using undocumented APIs. Direct API usage is a hallmark of sophisticated malware that aims to evade userland hooking employed by endpoint security products.

name: Scheduled Task/Job
description: Adversaries abuse task scheduling functionality built into operating systems to facilitate initial or recurring execution of malicious payloads. Scheduled tasks and cron jobs provide a reliable execution mechanism that persists across reboots and can be configured to run with elevated privileges, on specific triggers, or at timed intervals. This technique serves dual purposes: execution of payloads and persistence on compromised systems.

name: Scheduled Task
description: Adversaries create or modify Windows Scheduled Tasks using schtasks.exe, the Task Scheduler COM interface, or PowerShell cmdlets to execute malicious payloads on a recurring basis or at system startup. Tasks can be created remotely via RPC for lateral movement and configured to run as SYSTEM for privilege escalation.

name: Cron
description: Adversaries abuse cron jobs on Linux and macOS to schedule recurring execution of malicious scripts or binaries. Cron entries in /etc/crontab, /var/spool/cron, or user-specific crontabs provide persistent execution that survives system reboots and can run with the permissions of the owning user.

name: Windows Management Instrumentation
description: Adversaries abuse Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute commands and payloads on local and remote systems. WMI provides a powerful interface for querying system information, process creation (Win32_Process Create), and event subscription. Tools like wmic.exe, Invoke-WmiMethod, and Impacket's wmiexec.py enable remote execution without dropping files to disk. WMI process creation is commonly logged in Sysmon Event ID 1 with WmiPrvSE.exe as the parent process.

name: System Services
description: Adversaries interact with system services to execute commands or programs. Services provide execution with elevated privileges and can be used for both local and remote execution. The Windows Service Control Manager (SCM) can be used to create, modify, or start services that execute attacker-controlled binaries, while systemd on Linux provides similar service management capabilities.

name: Service Execution
description: Adversaries use the Windows Service Control Manager (sc.exe, SC_HANDLE API) to create or manipulate services that execute malicious commands or payloads with SYSTEM privileges. PsExec and Impacket's smbexec leverage this technique for remote execution by creating temporary services on target systems.

name: Inter-Process Communication
description: Adversaries abuse inter-process communication (IPC) mechanisms to execute commands within other processes, evade detection, and bypass security controls. IPC methods include COM objects, DDE (Dynamic Data Exchange), named pipes, and shared memory. By executing code within a trusted process's context, adversaries can inherit its privileges and avoid process-based detection rules.

name: Component Object Model
description: Adversaries abuse the Component Object Model (COM) to execute code through various interfaces including ShellBrowserWindow, MMC20.Application, and Excel.Application. COM objects can be instantiated locally or remotely (DCOM) for lateral movement, and execution occurs within the context of the COM server process, complicating attribution.

name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
description: Adversaries configure system settings to automatically execute programs during system boot or user logon, providing persistent access that survives reboots. This encompasses a wide range of OS mechanisms including Registry Run keys, Startup folders, init scripts, launchd plists, logon scripts, and authentication package DLLs. These autostart locations are among the most common persistence mechanisms observed in real-world intrusions because they reliably execute on every boot/logon cycle with minimal complexity.

name: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder
description: Adversaries add programs to Registry Run Keys (HKCU/HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run) or the user/system Startup folder for persistence. This is one of the most common and easily implemented persistence techniques, used by both commodity malware and sophisticated threat actors. It survives reboots and executes in the user or SYSTEM context depending on the hive used.

name: Winlogon Helper DLL
description: Adversaries abuse Winlogon notification packages and helper DLLs by modifying Registry keys under HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon to load malicious DLLs during the Windows logon process, executing with SYSTEM privileges before the user desktop loads.

name: Shortcut Modification
description: Adversaries modify shortcut (.lnk) files in the Startup folder, Desktop, or Taskbar to reference malicious executables or add malicious parameters (such as loading a DLL via rundll32). Users trigger execution by clicking the familiar shortcut without noticing the modified target path.

name: Create Account
description: Adversaries create new accounts on local systems, domain controllers, or cloud platforms to maintain persistent access to victim environments. Created accounts may use names that blend with existing naming conventions to avoid detection by administrators. Attackers may add accounts to privileged groups (Domain Admins, Azure Global Admins) or create accounts with specific access permissions needed for their objectives. Account creation generates auditable events that should be monitored for unauthorized additions.

name: Local Account
description: Adversaries create local accounts on compromised systems using net user, useradd, or PowerShell cmdlets and may add them to the local Administrators group. Local accounts provide persistent access to individual systems and can serve as fallback access if domain credentials are reset during incident response.

name: Domain Account
description: Adversaries create Active Directory domain accounts for enterprise-wide persistence. Domain accounts provide access across all domain-joined systems, can be added to privileged groups, and may be configured with long or non-expiring passwords to maintain long-term access even if other compromise indicators are remediated.

name: Cloud Account
description: Adversaries create cloud accounts (AWS IAM users, Azure AD/Entra ID accounts, GCP service accounts) for persistence in cloud environments. Cloud accounts can be provisioned with programmatic access keys that bypass MFA requirements and provide API-level access to cloud resources.

name: Account Manipulation
description: Adversaries manipulate existing accounts to maintain and/or elevate access to victim systems. This includes adding credentials to service principals, modifying group memberships, changing account permissions, resetting passwords, adding MFA devices under attacker control, and modifying account properties. Account manipulation is often preferred over account creation because changes to existing accounts generate less suspicion than new account creation events.

name: Additional Cloud Credentials
description: Adversaries add additional credentials (access keys, certificates, federated identity providers) to existing cloud accounts or service principals. In Azure AD, attackers may add certificates to application registrations or configure federated identity credentials, providing persistent access that survives password resets.

name: SSH Authorized Keys
description: Adversaries add their public SSH keys to the ~/.ssh/authorized_keys file on compromised Linux/Unix systems, enabling passwordless persistent access via SSH. This technique is difficult to detect through network monitoring since SSH traffic is encrypted, and the keys persist until manually removed.

name: Create or Modify System Process
description: Adversaries create or modify system-level processes (Windows services, systemd units, launchd daemons) to repeatedly execute malicious payloads with elevated privileges. System processes run independently of user sessions, start automatically at boot, and can be configured with automatic restart on failure. This provides highly reliable persistence that operates even when no user is logged in, making it ideal for maintaining access to servers and infrastructure.

name: Windows Service
description: Adversaries create new Windows services or modify existing ones (via sc.exe, New-Service, or Registry manipulation) to execute malicious binaries or DLLs with SYSTEM privileges at boot time. Malicious services may be configured with names and descriptions that mimic legitimate Microsoft services to avoid detection during manual review.

name: Server Software Component
description: Adversaries install or modify server software components to establish persistent access to web servers, database servers, and other server infrastructure. This includes deploying web shells, backdooring IIS modules, installing malicious SQL Server CLR assemblies, and modifying Transport Agents in Exchange. Server-level persistence is particularly dangerous because it provides access to all data processed by the server and persists through application restarts and user session changes.

name: Web Shell
description: Adversaries deploy web shells (PHP, ASPX, JSP, Python scripts) on compromised web servers to maintain persistent remote access and command execution through HTTP/HTTPS requests. Web shells like China Chopper, TurkeyShell, and custom ASPX shells provide file management, command execution, database access, and network pivoting capabilities. Traffic to web shells blends with normal web traffic and is encrypted by TLS.

name: Event Triggered Execution
description: Adversaries establish persistence by configuring code to execute in response to specific system events, ensuring malicious payloads run when certain conditions are met rather than on a fixed schedule. Events include WMI subscriptions, AppInit DLLs, image file execution options, accessibility feature abuse, and shell extension handlers. Event-triggered persistence can be stealthy because the malicious code only runs when specific trigger conditions occur.

name: WMI Event Subscription
description: Adversaries register permanent WMI event subscriptions consisting of an event filter, event consumer, and filter-to-consumer binding that execute code when specific system events occur (e.g., process creation, user logon, timer interval). WMI persistence is fileless in nature and difficult to detect without querying WMI namespace objects directly.

name: Hijack Execution Flow
description: Adversaries hijack the way operating systems run programs to execute their own malicious payloads by exploiting how applications search for and load dependencies. This includes DLL search order hijacking, DLL side-loading, dylib hijacking, PATH interception, and LD_PRELOAD abuse. These techniques are particularly effective because they execute within the context of a trusted application, inheriting its privileges and evading process-based blocklists or application whitelisting controls.

name: DLL Search Order Hijacking
description: Adversaries place malicious DLLs in directories searched before the legitimate DLL location, exploiting the Windows DLL search order. When a vulnerable application loads the DLL by name without specifying a full path, it loads the attacker's DLL instead, executing malicious code within the trusted application's process context.

name: DLL Side-Loading
description: Adversaries plant malicious DLLs alongside legitimate signed applications that load them by name. This technique is widely used by APT groups who deploy a legitimate signed executable paired with a malicious DLL, providing execution within a trusted process and bypassing application whitelisting solutions.

name: Modify Authentication Process
description: Adversaries modify authentication mechanisms and credential validation processes to intercept credentials, bypass authentication, or enable unauthorized access. This includes installing password filter DLLs that capture plaintext passwords during changes, patching domain controller authentication (Skeleton Key), modifying PAM modules on Linux, and compromising Active Directory Federation Services (ADFS) token-signing certificates (Golden SAML). These modifications are extremely dangerous because they affect the foundational trust of authentication infrastructure.

name: Domain Controller Authentication
description: Adversaries patch the LSASS process on domain controllers to install a skeleton key that allows authentication with any password alongside the legitimate password (Mimikatz misc::skeleton). This provides universal backdoor access to all domain accounts without changing passwords, though it does not survive DC reboots unless paired with additional persistence.

name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
description: Adversaries circumvent mechanisms designed to control elevated privileges, such as UAC on Windows, sudo on Linux, or Gatekeeper on macOS. By bypassing these controls, attackers obtain higher-level permissions without triggering consent prompts or authorization dialogs. Hundreds of UAC bypass techniques have been documented, many leveraging auto-elevating Windows binaries, DLL hijacking of elevated COM objects, or environment variable manipulation. Sudo misconfigurations (NOPASSWD, wildcard paths) and setuid binary abuse are common on Linux.

name: Bypass User Account Control
description: Adversaries bypass Windows UAC to elevate from medium to high integrity level without triggering the consent prompt. Common techniques include DLL hijacking of auto-elevating COM objects, fodhelper.exe environment variable manipulation, eventvwr.exe Registry hijack, and leveraging legitimate Windows utilities that auto-elevate. The UACME project documents over 70 known bypass techniques.

name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
description: Adversaries exploit software vulnerabilities in operating system kernels, drivers, or privileged services to escalate from low-privilege to SYSTEM, root, or administrator access. Common targets include Windows kernel vulnerabilities (print spooler, win32k, CLFS driver), Linux kernel exploits (Dirty Pipe, Dirty COW, overlayfs), and vulnerabilities in privileged services. Exploit frameworks like Metasploit include modules for many known privilege escalation CVEs, and purpose-built tools like PrintSpoofer, JuicyPotato, and GodPotato target specific Windows service account privilege escalation paths.

name: Access Token Manipulation
description: Adversaries modify Windows access tokens to operate under a different user or system security context, enabling privilege escalation and defense evasion. Techniques include token impersonation (using DuplicateToken to copy a privileged token), token theft from running processes, creating processes with stolen tokens (CreateProcessWithTokenW), and adjusting token privileges (SeDebugPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege). Tools like Incognito, Tokenvator, and the Cobalt Strike steal_token command facilitate these operations.

name: Token Impersonation/Theft
description: Adversaries steal or impersonate tokens from processes running under higher-privileged accounts using tools like Incognito's list_tokens/impersonate_token, Cobalt Strike's steal_token, or custom DuplicateTokenEx implementations. Service accounts with SeImpersonatePrivilege (common for IIS/SQL) are prime targets for potato-family exploits.

name: SID-History Injection
description: Adversaries inject SID-History attribute values into Active Directory accounts using Mimikatz's sid::add command or DCSync-based techniques. By adding the Domain Admins SID (S-1-5-21-domain-512) to a regular user's SID History, the attacker gains domain admin privileges without being a member of the Domain Admins group, which evades group membership auditing.

name: Domain Policy Modification
description: Adversaries modify domain-wide policies including Group Policy Objects (GPOs) and domain trust settings to escalate privileges, distribute malware, or weaken security controls across the entire Active Directory environment. GPO manipulation can push malicious scheduled tasks, modify security settings, or deploy software to all domain-joined systems simultaneously. Domain trust modifications can establish rogue trust relationships for cross-domain access.

name: Group Policy Modification
description: Adversaries modify Group Policy Objects to push malicious configurations, scheduled tasks, or startup scripts to domain-joined systems. Tools like SharpGPOAbuse automate GPO manipulation to add computer startup scripts, create immediate scheduled tasks, or modify security settings across organizational units.

name: Escape to Host
description: Adversaries exploit vulnerabilities or misconfigurations to break out of container isolation (Docker, Kubernetes pods) and access the underlying host operating system. Techniques include exploiting kernel vulnerabilities from within containers, abusing mounted sockets (Docker socket /var/run/docker.sock), leveraging excessive Linux capabilities (CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_NET_ADMIN), exploiting cgroup escape paths, and abusing privileged container configurations. Container escape provides full host access and potentially access to all other containers on the same node.

name: Impair Defenses
description: Adversaries maliciously modify or disable security tools and defensive mechanisms to avoid detection throughout their operation. This includes killing or suspending EDR/AV processes, modifying firewall rules, disabling event logging services, unloading ETW providers, patching AMSI in memory, and disabling audit policies. Sophisticated attackers use kernel-level techniques such as loading vulnerable drivers (BYOVD - Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) to terminate protected security processes. Impairing defenses is often one of the first post-exploitation actions taken by ransomware operators.

name: Disable or Modify Tools
description: Adversaries disable or modify security tools including antivirus (Defender, CrowdStrike, SentinelOne), EDR agents, SIEM forwarders, and monitoring software. Techniques include killing processes, modifying service configurations, BYOVD kernel driver attacks (e.g., Process Explorer's procexp.sys, Dell's dbutil_2_3.sys), and AMSI bypass through in-memory patching of amsi.dll.

name: Disable Windows Event Logging
description: Adversaries disable Windows event logging by stopping the EventLog service, patching ETW providers in memory (using tools like Invoke-Phant0m), clearing log channels, or modifying audit policies. This eliminates the telemetry that SIEM systems and forensic investigators rely on to detect and reconstruct attack activities.

name: Disable or Modify System Firewall
description: Adversaries disable or modify the Windows Firewall (netsh advfirewall set allprofiles state off), iptables, or cloud security group rules to allow malicious traffic for C2, lateral movement, or data exfiltration that would otherwise be blocked by network security controls.

name: Indicator Removal
description: Adversaries delete, modify, or overwrite artifacts generated on systems and networks to remove evidence of their presence and activities. This includes clearing event logs, deleting command history files, timestomping modified files, removing malware artifacts, and overwriting disk sectors. Indicator removal is typically performed during the final stages of an operation or continuously throughout to maintain operational security. Forensic investigation becomes significantly more difficult when indicators have been systematically removed.

name: Clear Windows Event Logs
description: Adversaries clear Windows Event Logs using wevtutil cl, Clear-EventLog, or direct manipulation of .evtx files to remove evidence of intrusion activities. Clearing generates Event ID 1102 (Security log cleared) which itself is a high-fidelity alert indicator. Sophisticated attackers may selectively delete individual events rather than clearing entire logs.

name: File Deletion
description: Adversaries delete files left behind by their actions including malware binaries, output files, staging directories, and tool artifacts using commands like del, rm, SDelete (secure deletion), or cipher /w. Secure deletion tools overwrite file contents to prevent forensic recovery from unallocated disk space.

name: Obfuscated Files or Information
description: Adversaries make payloads and data difficult to discover or analyze by encoding, encrypting, compressing, or obfuscating files, scripts, and network traffic. Techniques include base64 encoding, XOR encryption, string concatenation in scripts, executable packing (UPX, custom packers), code virtualization (VMProtect, Themida), control flow flattening, and steganography. Obfuscation prevents static analysis signatures from matching and increases the time required for manual reverse engineering. Multi-layer obfuscation combining several techniques requires iterative analysis to fully deobfuscate.

name: Binary Padding
description: Adversaries add junk data or null bytes to binary files to change file hashes and evade hash-based detection, inflate file size past automated analysis limits, and alter entropy calculations that sandbox detonation systems may use to identify malicious executables.

name: Command Obfuscation
description: Adversaries obfuscate commands using character insertion (^, backtick), environment variable substitution, string concatenation, encoding (Base64, certutil), and interpreter-specific evasion tricks to bypass command-line logging and detection rules that match specific command patterns.

name: Masquerading
description: Adversaries manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate or benign to users and security controls. This includes renaming malware to match legitimate system processes (svchost.exe, explorer.exe), matching file locations of trusted software, using right-to-left override characters to disguise file extensions, spoofing file metadata and digital signatures, and using legitimate application icons. Masquerading exploits human trust in familiar names and locations and can bypass application whitelisting that relies on file names rather than cryptographic hashes.

name: Match Legitimate Name or Location
description: Adversaries name their executables and scripts after legitimate OS files (svchost.exe, conhost.exe, csrss.exe) and place them in similar directories to blend in during process listing and manual review. Defenders detect this by comparing process image paths against known-good locations and validating digital signatures.

name: Process Injection
description: Adversaries inject code into the address space of separate running processes to evade process-based defenses, elevate privileges, and access the target process's memory. Major injection techniques include classic DLL injection (VirtualAllocEx + WriteProcessMemory + CreateRemoteThread), process hollowing, APC injection, thread execution hijacking, and process doppelganging. Injecting into trusted processes like explorer.exe, svchost.exe, or signed applications helps bypass application whitelisting, EDR process monitoring, and firewall rules that allow traffic from specific processes.

name: Dynamic-link Library Injection
description: Adversaries inject malicious DLLs into running processes using the VirtualAllocEx, WriteProcessMemory, CreateRemoteThread API pattern or through SetWindowsHookEx. The injected DLL executes within the target process context, inheriting its privileges and bypassing process-based security controls.

name: Process Hollowing
description: Adversaries create a suspended legitimate process (e.g., svchost.exe), unmap its memory contents using NtUnmapViewOfSection, write malicious code into the hollowed memory space, then resume the process. The malicious code runs within what appears to be a legitimate signed process, evading EDR and application whitelisting.

name: System Binary Proxy Execution
description: Adversaries abuse signed, trusted system binaries (Living Off the Land Binaries - LOLBins) to proxy execution of malicious content, bypassing application whitelisting, digital signature enforcement, and EDR behavioral rules. The LOLBAS project documents hundreds of Windows binaries that can be abused for execution, file download, compilation, and UAC bypass. Common LOLBins include rundll32.exe, mshta.exe, regsvr32.exe, certutil.exe, msiexec.exe, and cmstp.exe. These legitimate binaries are typically signed by Microsoft and present on all Windows systems.

name: Rundll32
description: Adversaries abuse rundll32.exe to load and execute malicious DLLs, JavaScript, or COM scriptlets. Since rundll32 is a signed Microsoft binary present on all Windows systems, its execution is typically allowed by application whitelisting policies. It can also be used to execute arbitrary JavaScript via the javascript: protocol handler.

name: Mshta
description: Adversaries abuse mshta.exe to execute malicious .hta files, VBScript, and JScript from local paths or remote URLs. Mshta runs outside of Internet Explorer's security context, bypassing browser security controls, and can be used to execute arbitrary code through inline scripts or remote HTA content.

name: Regsvr32
description: Adversaries abuse regsvr32.exe to execute malicious DLLs and COM scriptlets (.sct files), including loading them from remote URLs (Squiblydoo technique). Regsvr32 is a signed Microsoft binary that can be used to bypass application whitelisting and execute arbitrary code through the DllRegisterServer export.

name: Subvert Trust Controls
description: Adversaries undermine security controls that warn users about executing untrusted programs or verify the integrity to executed code. This includes code signing with stolen or purchased certificates, bypassing Mark-of-the-Web protections (which trigger SmartScreen and Office Protected View), manipulating Gatekeeper on macOS, and installing root certificates to trust attacker-signed malware. Subverting trust controls is critical for delivering payloads that bypass automated security checks and user warning dialogs.

name: Code Signing
description: Adversaries create, acquire, or steal code signing certificates to sign their malware, making it appear legitimate and trusted by the operating system. Stolen EV certificates are particularly valuable as they may bypass SmartScreen reputation checks entirely. Certificates can be purchased from compromised CAs or stolen from software development companies.

name: Reflective Code Loading
description: Adversaries load code directly into process memory without writing files to disk using techniques like reflective DLL injection (Stephen Fewer's method), .NET Assembly.Load() from byte arrays, and in-memory PE loading. This fileless approach bypasses on-disk AV scanning and leaves minimal forensic artifacts, though it can be detected through memory scanning and ETW tracing.

name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
description: Adversaries decode or deobfuscate files and information that were encoded during delivery to reveal actual payloads. Common methods include using certutil -decode for base64, PowerShell [Convert]::FromBase64String, XOR decryption loops, compression utilities, and custom decoders embedded in dropper/loader malware. This separates the encoded delivery from the decoded execution to evade static analysis at the delivery stage.

name: File and Directory Permissions Modification
description: Adversaries modify file or directory permissions using icacls, chmod, chattr, or PowerShell Set-Acl to evade access control restrictions, allow execution of malicious files, or prevent security tools from scanning or quarantining files. Permission changes may also be used to make evidence files immutable or to restrict access to tools used by incident responders.

name: Hide Artifacts
description: Adversaries hide artifacts associated with their behaviors to evade detection by users, administrators, and security tools. Techniques include setting hidden file attributes, using NTFS Alternate Data Streams to stash payloads, hiding processes through rootkit techniques, creating hidden users, and storing data in Registry values. Hidden artifacts persist on the system without appearing in standard directory listings, process enumerations, or administrative interfaces.

name: Hidden Files and Directories
description: Adversaries set the hidden attribute on files and directories using attrib +h on Windows or dot-prefix on Linux (e.g., .malware/) to conceal them from standard directory listings. Users and scripts that don't enumerate hidden files will miss these artifacts during manual review.

name: Brute Force
description: Adversaries use systematic trial-and-error methods to guess credentials when passwords are unknown or partially known. Techniques include password guessing (targeted lists), password spraying (one password across many accounts), credential stuffing (breached pairs), and offline hash cracking (hashcat, John the Ripper with GPU acceleration). Brute force attacks target a wide range of services including RDP, SSH, OWA, VPN portals, SMB, and cloud identity providers. Account lockout policies, rate limiting, and behavioral detection are primary defensive controls.

name: Password Guessing
description: Adversaries guess passwords using targeted lists based on common patterns, organizational naming conventions, seasonal patterns (Summer2024!), and information gathered during reconnaissance. Tools like Hydra, Medusa, and Burp Intruder automate these attempts across various protocols and can incorporate custom wordlists.

name: Password Cracking
description: Adversaries crack password hashes offline using tools like Hashcat and John the Ripper with GPU acceleration, precomputed rainbow tables, and rule-based mutations. Cracking NTLM hashes, NTLMv2 challenge-responses, Kerberos tickets, and extracted database hashes can reveal plaintext passwords within minutes to hours depending on password complexity.

name: Password Spraying
description: Adversaries try a single commonly used password (e.g., 'Winter2024!', 'Password1') against many accounts before moving to the next password, staying below account lockout thresholds. This technique is highly effective against organizations without MFA, especially when targeting cloud identity providers (Azure AD/Entra ID, Okta) where lockout policies may be more lenient.

name: Credential Stuffing
description: Adversaries automate login attempts using stolen username-password pairs from third-party data breaches, exploiting password reuse across services. Tools like SentryMBA, OpenBullet, and custom scripts can test millions of credential pairs against target portals, and even a low success rate yields valuable access across a large credential set.

name: OS Credential Dumping
description: Adversaries dump credentials from the operating system to obtain password hashes, Kerberos tickets, plaintext passwords, and authentication tokens. This is a cornerstone technique that enables lateral movement and privilege escalation. On Windows, targets include LSASS process memory, the SAM database, the NTDS.dit Active Directory database, cached credentials, and DPAPI-protected data. On Linux, targets include /etc/shadow, SSH keys, and in-memory credential caches. Mimikatz, secretsdump.py, and nanodump are commonly used tools.

name: LSASS Memory
description: Adversaries dump the LSASS process memory to extract NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and potentially plaintext passwords using Mimikatz (sekurlsa::logonpasswords), ProcDump, comsvcs.dll MiniDump, nanodump, or direct memory reading. Credential Guard (virtualization-based security) protects LSASS memory on modern Windows configurations, and PPL (Protected Process Light) prevents unauthorized memory access.

name: Security Account Manager
description: Adversaries extract NTLM password hashes from the Windows SAM database (C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM) using tools like secretsdump.py, reg save, or Volume Shadow Copy access. SAM hashes can be cracked offline or used directly in pass-the-hash attacks for lateral movement.

name: NTDS
description: Adversaries extract all domain account credentials from the Active Directory ntds.dit database file on domain controllers using Volume Shadow Copy (vssadmin/ntdsutil), direct file access, or DCSync replication. This provides password hashes for every account in the domain, enabling total domain compromise.

name: DCSync
description: Adversaries use the MS-DRSR (Directory Replication Service) protocol to simulate a domain controller and request password hash replication from a real DC using Mimikatz's lsadump::dcsync or Impacket's secretsdump.py. This requires Replicating Directory Changes permissions (held by Domain/Enterprise Admins) and retrieves password hashes without touching the ntds.dit file directly.

name: /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow
description: Adversaries extract password hashes from /etc/shadow on Linux systems (requires root access) and use offline cracking with Hashcat or John the Ripper. The /etc/passwd file, readable by all users, provides username enumeration even without root access, useful for subsequent brute force or lateral movement.

name: Steal Application Access Token
description: Adversaries steal application access tokens—particularly OAuth tokens, JWT tokens, API keys, and cloud service tokens—to authenticate to APIs and services without needing the user's password. Token theft can occur through phishing consent grant attacks (OAuth app abuse), browser cookie/token extraction, and memory scraping of applications that hold tokens. Stolen cloud tokens (AWS STS, Azure AD) can provide extensive access to cloud resources and may be used from anywhere without triggering geographic anomaly alerts.

name: Unsecured Credentials
description: Adversaries search compromised systems for insecurely stored credentials in configuration files, scripts, environment variables, Registry values, browser storage, and application databases. Credentials commonly found include plaintext passwords in web.config, database connection strings, API keys in .env files, SSH private keys, and credentials stored in cloud metadata endpoints. Automated tools like LaZagne, Seatbelt, and SharpChrome systematically harvest credentials from dozens of known storage locations.

name: Credentials In Files
description: Adversaries search local file systems for files containing passwords, API keys, and secrets in plaintext. Common targets include .config files, .env files, bash_history, PowerShell transcript logs, unattended installation files (unattend.xml), database backup files, and scripts containing hardcoded credentials.

name: Private Keys
description: Adversaries search for private key files (.pem, .pfx, .key, id_rsa) that enable authentication to remote systems, decryption of encrypted data, code signing, or TLS impersonation. Private keys stored without passphrase protection are immediately usable, while passphrase-protected keys can be targeted with offline brute force cracking.

name: Group Policy Preferences
description: Adversaries find plaintext credentials stored in Group Policy Preference (GPP) XML files on SYSVOL shares using Get-GPPPassword, gpp-decrypt, or Metasploit modules. Although the cpassword encryption key was publicly disclosed by Microsoft in 2012 (MS14-025), organizations with legacy GPOs may still have these recoverable credentials exposed.

name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets
description: Adversaries steal or forge Kerberos authentication tickets to impersonate users, escalate privileges, and move laterally within Active Directory environments without needing plaintext passwords. Major techniques include Golden Ticket (forging TGTs with the KRBTGT hash), Silver Ticket (forging service tickets), Kerberoasting (requesting and cracking service account tickets), AS-REP Roasting (cracking accounts without pre-auth), and Diamond Ticket (modification of legitimate TGTs). Kerberos attacks are central to Active Directory compromise and can provide persistent domain-wide access.

name: Golden Ticket
description: Adversaries forge Kerberos Ticket Granting Tickets (TGTs) using the KRBTGT account hash (obtained via DCSync or ntds.dit extraction) and Mimikatz's kerberos::golden command. A Golden Ticket provides unrestricted access to any resource in the domain with arbitrary user identity and group membership, and remains valid until the KRBTGT password is rotated twice.

name: Silver Ticket
description: Adversaries forge Kerberos service tickets using a service account's NTLM hash to access specific services (e.g., CIFS, HTTP, MSSQL) without contacting the KDC. Silver Tickets are harder to detect than Golden Tickets because they don't generate TGT requests, but they're limited to the specific service whose hash was compromised.

name: Kerberoasting
description: Adversaries request Kerberos TGS tickets for service accounts with registered SPNs using tools like Rubeus (kerberoast), GetUserSPNs.py, or Invoke-Kerberoast, then crack the tickets offline with Hashcat. Service accounts often have weak passwords and elevated privileges, making Kerberoasting one of the most impactful credential access techniques in Active Directory environments.

name: AS-REP Roasting
description: Adversaries identify accounts configured with 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication' and request AS-REP responses that contain a portion encrypted with the user's password hash. These responses are crackable offline using Hashcat mode 18200 or John the Ripper, often revealing weak passwords for service or legacy accounts.

name: Steal Web Session Cookie
description: Adversaries steal web session cookies from browsers to hijack authenticated sessions without needing credentials or passing MFA challenges. Cookies can be extracted from browser databases (Chrome's Cookies SQLite DB protected by DPAPI), through XSS attacks, via infostealer malware, or using adversary-in-the-middle proxy tools like Evilginx2 and Modlishka. Stolen session cookies are particularly dangerous because they bypass all authentication controls including MFA.

name: Multi-Factor Authentication Interception
description: Adversaries intercept or bypass multi-factor authentication through various techniques including real-time phishing proxies (Evilginx2, Modlishka) that relay MFA challenges, SIM swapping to receive SMS codes, malware that captures TOTP codes from authenticator apps, and social engineering help desk staff to reset MFA. AiTM (Adversary-in-the-Middle) phishing attacks have become the primary technique for bypassing MFA at scale.

name: Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation
description: Adversaries spam users with MFA push notifications (MFA fatigue/prompt bombing) until the frustrated user approves one, or use social engineering to convince users the prompts are legitimate. This technique was notably used in the 2022 Uber breach. Defenses include number matching (user must type a code displayed on screen), geographic context in push notifications, and limiting the number of MFA prompts.

name: Adversary-in-the-Middle
description: Adversaries position themselves between two communicating parties to intercept, relay, and potentially modify network traffic. Techniques include ARP spoofing, DNS poisoning, LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning, WPAD abuse, rogue DHCP servers, and BGP hijacking. Man-in-the-middle positioning enables credential interception, session hijacking, and data manipulation. Tools like Responder, Bettercap, mitmproxy, and Ettercap automate these attacks on local networks.

name: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay
description: Adversaries use tools like Responder and Inveigh to spoof LLMNR and NBT-NS responses on local networks, capturing NTLMv2 hashes when clients attempt name resolution. Captured hashes can be cracked offline or immediately relayed to other services using ntlmrelayx.py for authentication without cracking (SMB relay, LDAP relay, HTTP relay).

name: Account Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate accounts to identify valid users, administrators, service accounts, and privileged identities on systems and domains. On Windows, tools include net user, net group, Get-ADUser, and BloodHound for Active Directory enumeration. On Linux, /etc/passwd and getent provide user listings. In cloud environments, APIs enumerate IAM users, roles, and service principals. Account discovery feeds into privilege escalation planning, lateral movement target selection, and credential attack prioritization.

name: Local Account
description: Adversaries enumerate local accounts using net user, wmic useraccount, or Get-LocalUser on Windows and cat /etc/passwd on Linux to identify local administrators, service accounts, and targets for privilege escalation or credential harvesting.

name: Domain Account
description: Adversaries enumerate Active Directory accounts using net user /domain, Get-ADUser, LDAP queries, or BloodHound's SharpHound collector to map users, group memberships, admin accounts, service accounts with SPNs (for Kerberoasting), and accounts with delegation permissions.

name: Cloud Account
description: Adversaries enumerate cloud accounts using cloud provider APIs (aws iam list-users, az ad user list, gcloud iam service-accounts list) to identify privileged accounts, service principals with excessive permissions, and targets for credential-based attacks in cloud environments.

name: Network Service Discovery
description: Adversaries scan networks to discover running services on remote hosts using tools like Nmap, masscan, and native OS utilities (Test-NetConnection, netcat). Service discovery identifies open ports, running applications (web servers, databases, RDP, SSH), and potential attack surfaces for exploitation or lateral movement. Adversaries frequently scan the local subnet post-compromise to understand the internal network environment before advancing further into the network.

name: Process Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate running processes to understand the software environment, identify security tools (AV, EDR, DLP agents), discover running applications that may contain credentials in memory, and identify potential injection targets. Commands include tasklist, Get-Process on Windows, and ps aux on Linux. Understanding which security tools are running helps adversaries select appropriate evasion techniques.

name: System Information Discovery
description: Adversaries gather detailed system information including OS version, architecture, patch level, hostname, domain membership, installed software, and hardware specifications. Commands like systeminfo, uname -a, hostname, and wmic os provide this intelligence. This information helps attackers select appropriate exploits, determine privilege escalation paths, and understand the target environment's defensive posture and configuration.

name: File and Directory Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate files and directories to locate sensitive data, credentials, configuration files, and other information of interest. Searches target common locations including user home directories, document folders, backup directories, web root directories, and configuration paths. Automated tools like Seatbelt and SharFinder systematically search for files containing passwords, sensitive documents, and data of intelligence value.

name: Permission Groups Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate group memberships and permission settings to understand privilege hierarchies and identify attack paths. Tools like BloodHound visualize complex Active Directory relationships, revealing paths from compromised accounts to Domain Admin through nested group memberships, delegated permissions, session data, and ACL-based attack paths. Understanding group structure is essential for planning privilege escalation strategies.

name: Domain Groups
description: Adversaries enumerate domain security groups using net group /domain, Get-ADGroup, LDAP queries, or BloodHound to identify privileged groups (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins), their members, and nested group relationships that may create unexpected privilege paths.

name: Remote System Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate remote systems on the network to identify potential targets for lateral movement using net view, ping sweeps, ARP scanning, LDAP queries for computer objects, and DNS enumeration. Knowledge of other systems in the environment helps attackers plan their lateral movement path toward high-value targets like domain controllers, file servers, and database servers.

name: System Network Configuration Discovery
description: Adversaries collect network configuration details including IP addresses, DNS servers, DHCP settings, routing tables, network interfaces, proxy configurations, and VPN connections using ipconfig, ifconfig, route print, and netstat. This information reveals network segmentation, identifies potential egress paths for C2 and exfiltration, and highlights adjacent network segments for lateral movement.

name: System Network Connections Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate active network connections and listening ports using netstat, ss, Get-NetTCPConnection, or conntrack to discover communicated-with systems, identify other hosts they can pivot to, and understand the network topology from the perspective of the compromised system.

name: System Owner/User Discovery
description: Adversaries identify the primary user, currently logged-in users, and interactive session information using whoami, quser, w, and query user commands. Understanding who uses a system helps identify high-value targets (administrators, executives) and determine whether the compromised workstation provides access to valuable data or privileged sessions.

name: Domain Trust Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate Active Directory domain and forest trust relationships using nltest /domain_trusts, Get-ADTrust, or BloodHound to identify trust paths that enable cross-domain and cross-forest lateral movement. Trust relationships between domains, especially bidirectional trusts, can provide access to entirely separate AD environments through SID History injection, trust ticket forging, or credential reuse.

name: Group Policy Discovery
description: Adversaries gather information about Group Policy settings using gpresult, Get-GPO, or direct SYSVOL examination to identify security configurations, deployment scripts, mapped drives, credential policies, and misconfigurations across the domain. GPO analysis reveals password policies, audit settings, software deployment methods, and potential persistence opportunities.

name: Cloud Infrastructure Discovery
description: Adversaries discover cloud infrastructure components including virtual machines, storage accounts, databases, networking configurations, and identity services using cloud provider APIs and CLI tools (aws ec2 describe-instances, az vm list, gcloud compute instances list). This enumeration reveals the full cloud attack surface and helps identify misconfigured resources, public-facing assets, and high-value data stores.

name: Cloud Service Dashboard
description: Adversaries use cloud service dashboards and management consoles (AWS Console, Azure Portal, GCP Console) to visually discover resources, configurations, permissions, and network architecture. Dashboard access provides a comprehensive view of the cloud environment that may reveal misconfigured resources not visible through CLI-based enumeration.

name: Cloud Service Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate available cloud services and their configurations to identify services that can be abused for execution, data access, or persistence, such as Lambda functions, storage buckets, databases, serverless computing, and container services across AWS, Azure, and GCP environments.

name: System Service Discovery
description: Adversaries enumerate services running on local and remote systems using sc query, Get-Service, systemctl list-units, and WMIC to identify running applications, security software, database services, and web servers. Service enumeration reveals potential lateral movement targets, injection opportunities, and services that may be exploitable.

name: System Time Discovery
description: Adversaries gather system time information using time, w32tm, and date commands to synchronize scheduled execution of malicious activities, understand time zones for targeting specific work hours, set correct timestamps when manipulating files (timestomping), and coordinate multi-system operations.

name: Remote Services
description: Adversaries use valid credentials to authenticate to remote services for lateral movement across the network. This includes RDP, SMB/Windows admin shares, SSH, WinRM/PSRemoting, VNC, and cloud management APIs. Once authenticated, attackers can execute commands, transfer files, and establish persistence on additional systems. Lateral movement through legitimate remote services is difficult to distinguish from normal administrative activity, making behavioral analytics and anomaly detection essential for identifying unauthorized access patterns.

name: Remote Desktop Protocol
description: Adversaries use RDP (port 3389) to interactively control remote Windows systems using stolen credentials, including both GUI sessions and restricted admin/remote credential guard modes. RDP provides full desktop access, is commonly allowed through firewalls for administration, and can be tunneled through compromised systems using tools like Chisel or SSH port forwarding.

name: SMB/Windows Admin Shares
description: Adversaries connect to Windows admin shares (C$, ADMIN$, IPC$) via SMB (port 445) using stolen credentials or pass-the-hash to copy files, execute commands via SCM/WMI, and move laterally. Tools like PsExec, smbexec, and wmiexec.py leverage SMB shares for remote file staging and service-based execution.

name: SSH
description: Adversaries use SSH to authenticate to remote Linux, macOS, and network devices using stolen passwords, private keys, or SSH agent forwarding for lateral movement. SSH tunneling (-L, -R, -D flags) additionally enables port forwarding and SOCKS proxying through compromised hosts to reach otherwise inaccessible network segments.

name: Windows Remote Management
description: Adversaries use WinRM (ports 5985/5986) and PowerShell Remoting (Enter-PSSession, Invoke-Command) for lateral movement and remote execution on Windows systems. WinRM provides scriptable remote management, enabling execution of PowerShell commands across multiple systems simultaneously, file transfers, and persistent remote sessions.

name: Use Alternate Authentication Material
description: Adversaries use non-password authentication material including NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, application access tokens, and web session cookies to authenticate and move laterally without knowing the user's plaintext password. Pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, and token-based authentication bypass password-based controls and often circumvent MFA since the authentication material represents an already-authenticated session. These techniques are fundamental to post-exploitation Active Directory tradecraft.

name: Pass the Hash
description: Adversaries use stolen NTLM password hashes to authenticate via pass-the-hash (PtH) to Windows services that accept NTLM authentication, without needing the plaintext password. Tools like Mimikatz (sekurlsa::pth), Impacket's psexec/smbexec, and CrackMapExec enable PtH across SMB, WMI, and other Windows protocols. Local administrator password reuse across hosts makes PtH devastating in pre-LAPS environments.

name: Pass the Ticket
description: Adversaries inject stolen or forged Kerberos tickets (TGTs or TGS tickets) into their session using Mimikatz (kerberos::ptt) or Rubeus (ptt) to authenticate to services as the ticket's principal. Pass-the-ticket enables lateral movement using Kerberos authentication, which may be the only option when NTLM is restricted by network policies.

name: Lateral Tool Transfer
description: Adversaries transfer tools, scripts, and malware between systems within a compromised environment using SMB file shares, RDP clipboard/drive redirection, SCP/SFTP, PowerShell remoting, or native file copy utilities. This distributes attack tools without requiring external downloads that might trigger network security alerts.

name: Internal Spearphishing
description: Adversaries send targeted phishing messages from already-compromised internal email accounts to other employees, leveraging the trust inherent in internal communications. Internal spearphishing is highly effective because it bypasses external email security controls, comes from a known colleague's account, and may reference legitimate ongoing projects or conversations.

name: Exploitation of Remote Services
description: Adversaries exploit vulnerabilities in network services running on remote systems for lateral movement, including SMB (EternalBlue/MS17-010), RDP (BlueKeep/CVE-2019-0708), Apache Struts, and internal web application vulnerabilities. Unlike credential-based lateral movement, exploitation provides access without requiring valid credentials but may generate more detectable artifacts.

name: Remote Service Session Hijacking
description: Adversaries hijack existing authenticated remote service sessions (RDP sessions via tscon.exe session stealing, SSH connection hijacking) rather than creating new sessions. Session hijacking provides access using an already-authenticated session, bypassing credential requirements and potentially gaining access with higher privileges from an active admin session.

name: Archive Collected Data
description: Adversaries compress and/or encrypt collected data prior to exfiltration to reduce transfer volume, evade DLP inspection, and avoid content-based detection rules. This typically occurs during the staging phase before exfiltration. Common tools include 7-Zip, WinRAR, tar/gzip on Linux, and PowerShell's Compress-Archive.
